my_sentence: No bad things happen.
correction: Nothing bad happened.
explanation: You were reporting about a finished day, so use the past simple 'happened'. Also prefer the single word 'nothing' over 'no bad things' for a more natural negative.
correct_examples: Nothing bad happened today.
Nothing went wrong during the upgrade.
context: Teacher asked why Jack looked gloomy

my_sentence: No one want to apply it.
correction: No one will apply for it.
explanation: You 'apply for' a job/position — the preposition 'for' is required before the object. This recurred today (you also said 'I will apply it'). Note 'no one' takes a singular verb.
correct_examples: She applied for the job last week.
I'll apply for it tomorrow.
context: Talking about government jobs in the countryside vs. city

my_sentence: If a position is in the city center, many people will apply; however, if it in the countryside, no one will apply.
correction: If a position is in the city center, many people will apply for it; if it is in the countryside, no one will apply.
explanation: Every clause needs a verb — 'if it in the countryside' is missing 'is'. Keep the parallel 'if ..., (then) ...' structure for both cases instead of inserting 'however' between them.
correct_examples: If it is in the city, people apply; if it is far away, no one does.
If the job is well paid, applicants come; if it isn't, they don't.
context: Comparing city-center and countryside government positions

my_sentence: If a company can't change their strategies quickly, and he will go bankrupt.
correction: If a company can't change its strategies quickly, it will go bankrupt.
explanation: A company is referred to with 'it/its', not 'he'. Also don't put 'and' before the main clause of an if-sentence — the if-clause already links the two.
correct_examples: If a company can't adapt, it will fail.
The firm changed its strategy, so it survived.
context: Why companies cut their workforce

my_sentence: After Covid-19, most company run out of their money.
correction: After Covid-19, most companies ran out of money.
explanation: 'most' + a countable noun takes the plural ('companies'), and a finished past event needs the past tense 'ran'.
correct_examples: Most companies cut costs that year.
Many firms ran out of cash during the pandemic.
context: Why workforce cutbacks became common

my_sentence: So the situation become more serious.
correction: So the situation became more serious.
explanation: You were describing a past development, so use the past simple 'became'.
correct_examples: The situation became serious after the layoffs.
Things became harder once sales dropped.
context: Effect of Covid-19 on companies

my_sentence: We regarded economy is important by that time.
correction: We regarded the economy as important at that time.
explanation: The verb 'regard' uses the pattern 'regard something AS something', not 'regard ... is'. Add the article 'the economy', and use 'at that time' for a point in the past. (assistant note)
correct_examples: We regarded the economy as our top priority.
They regarded him as a good leader.
context: When China's economy recovered

my_sentence: Being a doctor, is it respected by others?
correction: If you're a doctor, most people will respect you.
explanation: The passive 'is it respected by others' sounds awkward; it's more natural to make people the subject of an active sentence.
correct_examples: If you're a teacher, students respect you.
People respect doctors in my country.
context: Whether doctors are respected

my_sentence: If you don't effort, you can't catch them.
correction: If you don't make an effort, you can't seize them.
explanation: 'effort' is a noun, not a verb — say 'make an effort'. And you 'seize/grab' an opportunity rather than 'catch' it. (assistant note)
correct_examples: If you don't make an effort, you'll fall behind.
You have to seize every opportunity.
context: What to do to avoid losing your job

my_sentence: If he or she can responsibility, that will be pretty good.
correction: If he or she can be responsible, that's pretty good.
explanation: After the modal 'can', use 'be + adjective (responsible)', not the noun 'responsibility'. ('be' + adjective describes the person.)
correct_examples: A good worker can be responsible.
She can be very reliable when needed.
context: What makes someone a good worker

my_sentence: Volunteer is not popular in my country.
correction: Volunteering is not popular in my country.
explanation: When the subject is the activity itself, use the gerund 'Volunteering', not the bare verb/noun 'volunteer'.
correct_examples: Volunteering is common among students.
Recycling is encouraged here.
context: Whether volunteering is popular

my_sentence: I have never do anything for community service.
correction: I have never done anything for community service.
explanation: The present perfect needs the past participle 'done' after 'have never', not the base form 'do'.
correct_examples: I have never done that before.
She has never worked abroad.
context: Whether Jack has done community service

my_sentence: Most people know me.
correction: Most of them know me.
explanation: You'd just mentioned the people in your office, so replace the repeated noun with a pronoun ('them') to avoid repetition. The teacher reminded you of this twice today.
correct_examples: My colleagues are friendly; most of them know me.
I've met the team, and most of them remember me.
context: Whether everyone at work knows Jack

my_sentence: Even you do the same thing, you can earn more in the sales department.
correction: Even if you do the same thing, you can earn more in the sales department.
explanation: To introduce a hypothetical, use 'even if' (or 'even though' for a real fact) — not bare 'even'. This recurred several times today.
correct_examples: Even if you work hard, choices matter more.
Even though it's cheap, it sells well.
context: Why the sales department pays more

my_sentence: Most companies that sell product to customers depend on marketing.
correction: Most companies that sell products to customers depend on marketing.
explanation: Countable nouns used in a general sense take the plural — 'products' (and likewise 'coupons'). The teacher corrected this several times today.
correct_examples: They sell products online.
The store hands out coupons.
context: Which companies rely on marketing

my_sentence: They want to recruit some idols or peoples.
correction: They want to recruit some idols or people.
explanation: 'people' is already the plural of 'person', so never add -s to make 'peoples' (except 'peoples' = different ethnic groups/nations).
correct_examples: They recruit talented people.
Many people applied for the role.
context: Comparing the entertainment industry with networking

my_sentence: We don't have a share department.
correction: We don't have a sales department.
explanation: 'sales' (/seɪlz/) kept coming out as 'share'. The team that sells is the 'sales department' — keep the s/l sound distinct from 'sh'.
correct_examples: She works in the sales department.
Our sales team met the client.
context: Whether Jack's company has a sales department

my_sentence: The products can be sure on the shelf.
correction: The products can be shown on the shelf.
explanation: 'shown' (the past participle of 'show', /ʃoʊn/) was coming out as 'sure'/'shoe'. Use 'can be + past participle': 'can be shown'.
correct_examples: These items can be shown on the top shelf.
The data can be shown in a chart.
context: Who decides which products are displayed in stores

my_sentence: Filipino Peso is the smallest unit.
correction: The Philippine peso is the smallest currency; the smallest currency unit is the cent.
explanation: Money is a 'currency', not a 'unit'. The smallest subdivision of a currency is the 'cent' (e.g. 100 cents = 1 peso).
correct_examples: The yen is Japan's currency.
One hundred cents make a dollar.
context: Comparing currencies of China, Japan and the Philippines

my_sentence: You can pay one point nine nine.
correction: You can pay nine ninety-nine. (9.99)
explanation: When reading an amount, don't say the cents digit by digit. Read the decimal part as a whole number: 9.99 = 'nine ninety-nine', 19.99 = 'nineteen ninety-nine'. (Reading digit by digit is for codes, plate numbers and addresses, not amounts.)
correct_examples: It costs nineteen ninety-nine.
The total is twelve fifty.
context: Talking about prices ending in .99
